proposed_short: The remote past: a simple (one-word) past tense for completed events, used in literary narrative, formal writing, and much southern speech where the north uses the passato prossimo.
proposed_long: The passato remoto, the remote past, narrates a completed past event as a single bounded whole: 'Dante nacque nel 1265' (Dante was born in 1265). Unlike the passato prossimo it is one word, not a compound, and unlike the imperfect it states the event rather than describing an ongoing background. It is the default past of novels, history, and biography, and the everyday spoken past across much of southern Italy, while northern and standard spoken Italian mostly uses the passato prossimo. Its formation is famously irregular: the -are and -ire classes are tidy (parlai, dormii), regular -ere verbs accept two endings (credei or credetti), a large class of -ere verbs has a strong 1-3-3 stem (presi, prese, presero, but regular prendesti, prendemmo, prendeste), and a handful are wholly suppletive (fui, ebbi, feci). Reading it is a B2 skill; producing it is C1.
rationale: The topic's own name, used throughout this batch's explanations and absent from the glossary, which has passato prossimo, trapassato prossimo, imperfect indicative, future and conditional but no remote past. The aliases catch the common English names (past historic, preterite).

proposed_short: An irregular passato remoto stem that appears in only three persons (io, lui/lei, loro), as prendere's pres- in presi / prese / presero, while the other three persons stay regular (prendesti, prendemmo, prendeste).
proposed_long: A large class of Italian -ere verbs forms the passato remoto on a 'strong' stem in the first singular, third singular, and third plural only, taking the endings -i, -e, -ero directly on that stem: prendere gives presi, prese, presero. The second singular, first plural, and second plural keep the ordinary stem and regular endings: prendesti, prendemmo, prendeste. This 1-3-3 alternation is the defining difficulty of the passato remoto. Two errors mark a learner who has not internalised it: carrying the strong stem into the regular middle persons (presesti for prendesti) and regularising the strong persons (prendei for presi). The high-frequency strong verbs include prendere (presi), scrivere (scrissi), vedere (vidi), mettere (misi), chiedere (chiesi), leggere (lessi), vivere (vissi) and venire (venni). Several otherwise suppletive verbs also use a strong stem in these three persons, notably avere (ebbi, ebbe, ebbero).
rationale: The central concept of the largest leaf, used in most strong-class and suppletive explanations. The glossary has 'stem', 'stem change' and 'stem expansion' but nothing for the person-restricted strong stem of the passato remoto. The '1-3-3 pattern' alias matches the dispatch's own shorthand.

proposed_short: The remote pluperfect: the passato remoto of the auxiliary plus a past participle (ebbe finito, fu partito), used only in a subordinate clause after a temporal conjunction whose main clause is itself in the passato remoto.
proposed_long: The trapassato remoto is the rare, literary compound that sets one completed action immediately before another in remote-past narrative. It is built from the passato remoto of avere or essere plus the past participle: 'Appena ebbe finito di parlare, tutti applaudirono' (as soon as he had finished speaking, everyone applauded). It appears almost only in a subordinate clause introduced by appena, dopo che, or quando, whose main clause is in the passato remoto. Auxiliary choice and participle agreement follow the same rules as the passato prossimo (essere for motion and change-of-state verbs, with agreement; avere otherwise). In everyday Italian its work is done by the trapassato prossimo (aveva finito) or the passato prossimo, so for most learners it is a recognition-only form at C1-C2.
rationale: The rarest leaf's tense name, used in its explanations. The glossary has trapassato prossimo but not its remote counterpart; the aliases give the English names a reader of older literature meets.
